title: Monthly average surface temperatures by decade
subtitle: The temperature of the air measured 2 meters above the ground, encompassing land, sea, and in-land water surfaces.
caption:
Monthly average surface temperatures by decade
The temperature of the air measured 2 meters above the ground, encompassing land, sea, and in-land water surfaces.

The line chart plots global monthly average surface temperatures across eight decades, ranging from the 1940s to the 2020s, with the vertical axis measuring temperature in degrees Celsius from 12°C to 17°C. The horizontal axis displays the months of the year, numbered 1 through 12 to represent January through December. Each decade is represented by a distinct line, with the 2020s shown in dark red at the top, followed by the 2010s, 2000s, 1990s, 1980s, 1970s, 1960s, and 1950s in progressively lighter shades, while the 1940s appears as a dark grey line at the bottom. All lines follow a consistent seasonal curve, starting near 12°C in January, rising to a peak in July, and falling back to roughly 12°C by December. The 2020s line sits highest throughout the year, reaching approximately 16.7°C in July, while the 1940s line remains lowest, peaking around 15.7°C. The 1950s, 1960s, and 1970s lines cluster closely together near the bottom of the range, generally staying between 12°C and 15.5°C. The 1980s, 1990s, 2000s, and 2010s form a middle group, with each successive decade positioned slightly higher than the last. The decadal average for the 2020s, comprising fewer than the typical 10 data points, may introduce greater uncertainty compared to other decades. The vertical spacing between the lines widens slightly during the summer months, showing larger temperature differences between decades in July than in January or December. The data shows a clear upward shift in monthly average temperatures with each passing decade from the 1940s through the 2020s.
